coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermally carbonized xylem sap for use in chemosensors, on and off switches, and memory devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Semeniuk, Maria
Tjong, Jimi
Lu, Zheng-Hong
Sain, Mohini
coredata.dc:description:
Multiple novel chemo sensor devices were created using hydrothermally synthesized fluorescent carbon produced from xylem sap. Most notably, UV light shone on the carbon can be used to selectively detect Fe3＋ ions and pH by the “naked eye” instantly, a first for xylem-syrup-derived hydrocarbons. A large stroke shift is present with outstanding photo-stability, sensitivity. NOT, AND, and NOR logic gates were derived using this novel behaviour, and implemented in proof-of-concept applications including an on–off switch, a chemo sensor, and a memory device based on the fundamentals of transistors and hydrochar’s structural attributes. For the first time, characteristic crystalline sp2 carbon was observed in hydrochar xylem syrup, the results confirm 0.34 nm interlayer spacing and Raman spectroscopy displays an
I
D
/
I
G
ratio of 0.22 similar to graphitic structures. This material designed chemo sensor devices based on logic gates which have a significant broad potential to act as smart nano devices and displace traditional metal–oxide–semiconductor field-effect transistor (MOSFET) based circuits. The field of renewable hydrocarbon is the subject of a considerable, widening range of interest for a diverse field of applications that can be applied in innovative batteries, sensors, smart materials, healthcare and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Xylem sap
Hydrochar
Chemosensor
on–off switch
Memory device

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanism to form fluorescent selective ion detecting in HC.

(a) Raman of carbonized maple syrup hydrothermal 180 °C grinded with liquid
N
2
, (b) TEM images maple syrup HC and the magnified rhomboidal graphite structure, (c) EDS results of HC maple syrup, and XRD of (d) HC maple syrup and (e) freeze dried maple syrup.
(a) FTIR spectra of HC maple syrup, and (b) UV–Vis and PL of HC maple syrup with an insert of PL under daylight and long UV light.

(a) Selectivity detection of various ions, (b) PL emission spectra of Fe3＋ ions at different concentrations, (c) the relationship between ion concentrations and photoluminescence intensity, (d) Stern–Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching, and (e) PL emission spectra of various pH, with insert of sample before and after HCl addition under UV light.
(a) SR latch and (b) memory device of chemo sensor.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermally carbonized xylem sap for use in chemosensors, on and off switches, and memory devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Biocomposites and Biomaterials Processing, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, M5S 3B3, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Biocomposites and Biomaterials Processing, University of Toronto

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Forestry and Conservation, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, M5S 3B3, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, M5S 3G8, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, M5S 3G8, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering, University of Toronto

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Material Science and Engineering, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario, M5S 3E3, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Multiple novel chemo sensor devices were created using hydrothermally synthesized fluorescent carbon produced from xylem sap. Most notably, UV light shone on the carbon can be used to selectively detect Fe3＋ ions and pH by the “naked eye” instantly, a first for xylem-syrup-derived hydrocarbons. A large stroke shift is present with outstanding photo-stability, sensitivity. NOT, AND, and NOR logic gates were derived using this novel behaviour, and implemented in proof-of-concept applications including an on–off switch, a chemo sensor, and a memory device based on the fundamentals of transistors and hydrochar’s structural attributes. For the first time, characteristic crystalline sp2 carbon was observed in hydrochar xylem syrup, the results confirm 0.34 nm interlayer spacing and Raman spectroscopy displays an
I
D
/
I
G
ratio of 0.22 similar to graphitic structures. This material designed chemo sensor devices based on logic gates which have a significant broad potential to act as smart nano devices and displace traditional metal–oxide–semiconductor field-effect transistor (MOSFET) based circuits. The field of renewable hydrocarbon is the subject of a considerable, widening range of interest for a diverse field of applications that can be applied in innovative batteries, sensors, smart materials, healthcare and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data available on request from the authors.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the last decade, fluorescent molecules have attracted considerable attention in medical and electronic applications (Semeniuk et al., 2019). A route to obtain fluorescence is hydrothermal carbonization. Hydrothermal conversion of biomass produces gas, aqueous intermediates and solid carbonaceous hydrochar (HC) (Ischia and Fiori, 2020). HC is formed in a process where heat is applied to biomass in a liquid solution at elevated temperatures and pressure ranging from 2–10 MPa for a prolonged period of time. Hydrothermal carbonization does not require that the biomass is dry prior to treatment, unlike other thermochemical procedures such as combustion, pyrolysis, and gasification. Hydrothermal carbonization has many other advantages, such as lower energy consumption and higher carbon product yield over pyrolysis (Kang et al., 2012; Libra et al., 2011). The resulting HC can be used in many applications such as fuel for power generation (Kambo and Dutta, 2015), in capacitors (Fang et al., 2018; Sanchez-Sanchez et al., 2020), as a soil amendment (Bargmann et al., 2014), as a catalyst for synthesis (Takeuchi et al., 2008), and in low-cost absorption of gas or contaminates (Ma et al., 2017).

Xylem sap is an environmentally sustainable carbon source, extracted from deciduous trees during the freeze thaw cycle. The sap contains up to 3% high quality sugars by volume, as well as trace amounts of phenolic compounds, primary amines, peptides, amino acids, proteins, and minerals (Alli et al., 1992). To the best of our knowledge, there has been no study on carbon product synthesis from the xylem sap precursor.

Some carbon allotropes, such as carbon dots and graphene-like structures, have been used to detect Fe3＋ ions in both biological and environmental systems (Georgakilas et al., 2015; Yi et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2021). As Fe ions are critical for cell function, including cell metabolism, oxygenation of blood, electron transfer systems and enzymatic co-factors, detection of these ions serve several critical functions in cell systems biology (Li et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2020). Additionally, iron is abundantly used in metal manufacturing production, since by mass iron is the most common element on Earth (Atchudan et al., 2018). Fluorescent detection of Fe3＋ ions have numerous advantages, such as excellent sensitivity, high selectivity, and ease of operation (Picard et al., 2019). In the field of computing, Boolean logic gates establish the basics of digital circuit implementation technologies, which use Boolean algebra to program logic devices. One alternative to silicon-based logic devices is to construct molecular logic devices. Molecular spin has been used to engineer different types of switches, sensors, etc. (Zhao and Chen, 2020; Zhao et al., 2021).

In the present work, xylem sap collected from Acer saccharum in the late winter/early spring was evaporated to produce syrup and then hydrothermally treated under heat and pressure to achieve a fluorescent HC. Different characterization techniques were performed on the carbonaceous material, and it was determined that it can be used to selectively detect Fe3＋ metal ions and pH. A possible mechanism was suggested in relation to metal ion detection and pH. Furthermore, an ON and OFF switch and memory device based on a chemosensor was designed.
Xylem sap was collected from sugar maple (Acer saccharum) trees in Oro-Medonte, ON, Canada, and was frozen until further processing. Maple syrup was produced heating sap until the water evaporated.

Ion salts, KCl, FeCl3, FeCl2, CuBr, CrCl3, and CaCl were dissolved in distilled water to obtain different ion concentration from 5 to
2000
μ m
ol L−1.
HCl and NaOH were used to change the pH of the solution.

The maple syrup was hydrothermally heated in a 300 mL PARR Instruments Company closed vessel system to a temperature of 180 °C for 7 h at a pressure of 10 bar. The solids was then filtered using a
0 . 1
μ m
polyethersulfone membrane filter from Sterlitech corporation. The solids were cryoground to obtain powder.

The photoluminescent (PL) of the samples were tested by using the F97Pro fluorospectrophotometer of Shanghai Lengguang Technology Ltd. To prepare for this test, 1 mg of the sample were dispersed in 10 mL water in cuvette and 5 nm slit widths were set. PL quantum yield (QY) was determined by the comparison to the control quinine sulphate solution using the following equation:
(1)
φ =
φ
r
×
I /
I
r
×
A
r
/ A
×
n
2
/
n
r
2
where the φ is the QY, I is the integrated PL emission intensity (excited at 355 nm), n is the refractive index (1.334 for deionized water), A is the absorbance value (less than 0.1) of UV–Vis absorbance at 355 nm (deionized water), and the subscript “r” refers to the standard. Quinine sulphate dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H
2
SO4 (
φ
r
= 0 . 54
) is selected as a standard control. The 0.02 g of HC maple syrup was dissolved in 40 mL of N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) from Sigma Aldrich. The detection of Fe3＋, Fe2＋, Cu2＋, K＋, Cr3＋, and Ca＋ ions was carried out at room temperature in aqueous solutions by measuring the fluorescence quenching of various concentrations of ions.

Both Raman Spectroscopy and Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) were used to determine the chemical composition. OPUS software was used to measure and process FT-IR and Raman Spectra. The FT-IR examination was processed in Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer, 1 mg of sample material has been mixed thoroughly with potassium bromide powder to make a 200 mg testing pellet by pressing using 5,000 kgpressure with a Carver model 4350-L pellet maker (maximum pressure: 10,000 Kg). The spectra range of the FT-IR spectrometer was 4000–400 cm​ −1 and the resolution was 1 cm−1. For the Raman analysis, the samples were studied using a Bruker Senterra Raman Microscope set at a laser of 20 mW with 532 nm wavelength of the excitation. The spectral range has chosen between 59 to 3700 cm−1 and the spectral resolution was set at 3 – 5 cm−1. The confocal optical microscope (OLYMPUS BX51) was adjusted to 20 x metallurgical microscope objective during the analysis. The integration time is 5 s, 10 co-addition.
Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns of were collected using a Bruker D2-Phaser Xray diffractometer (Cu Ka radiation at 30 kV).
High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) imaging was undertaken using the Hitachi STEM, SU9000.

Xylem sap obtained from A. saccharum trees is 95%–99% water and contains various macronutrients, micronutrients and phytochemicals. Sap flows in the early spring when the temperature fluctuates below freezing (−5 °C to −10 °C) at night and thaws during the day (Stuckel and Low, 1996). A detailed mechanism for graphite formation is found in Scheme 1. The major synthesis step includes a Maillard reaction occurring during the evaporation process, where the amino acids react with the reducing sugars to produce pyrazine and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) (Kermasha et al., 1995; Filipic et al., 1969). Caramelization reaction, also called polymerization, produces colour and degrades the sap, which produces flavour (Mistry, 2006). Caramelization is a process that involves heating sucrose, forming coloured compounds in the absence of water. This stepwise reaction removes water, then triggers polymerization, which results in brown coloured compounds of heterogeneous structure (Mistry, 2006). The heterogeneous structure has many different isomers formed with a common empirical structure. At 200 °C, the reaction has 3 separate stages. The first stage is heating until 4.5% of the weight is lost, and isosacchrosan is formed. The reaction follows: C12H22O11 – H2O → C12H20O10 (isosacchrosan). An isosacchrosan molecule is a sucrose with one less water. The second stage involves further heating, resulting in dimerization of sucrose and elimination of more water, forming caramelan. Caramelan, a brown, water-soluble pigment, is formed by the following reaction: 2 C12H22O11 – 4H2O → C24H36O18. The third stage involves further heating, eliminating even more water and causing trimerization of sucrose. At this point about 14% of the weight is lost, and another water-soluble pigment, brown caramelen, is formed. 3 C12H22O11 – 8H2O → C36H50O25. If further heating occurs, a dark brown pigment called humin or caramelin forms with a high molecular weight. Further heating results in the graphitization of sugars (Gupta et al., 2012).

We see the formation of a sharp G band at 1585 cm−1, suggesting the graphitization of syrup, and in plane vibration of sp2 hybridized HC (Carvalho et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2021). The D band at 1350 cm−1 suggests defect sites are presenting sp2 hybridized HC, most likely due to the presence of free graphene edges (Reich and Thomsen, 2004; Askari et al., 2021). The
I
D
/
I
G
ratio of 0.22 indicates the formation of a highly crystalline structure (Frank et al., 2011; Semeniuk et al., 2021b). The deconvoluted 2D feature shows multi layered graphene (Fig. 1a) (Ferrari, 2007). This result is consistent with HRTEM.

The HRTEM study is presented in Fig. 1b. It can be clearly seen that the HC graphite has a laminated structure and a rhomboidal stacking arrangement. This indicates that sucrose from the syrup was carbonized to form a crystal lattice structure. This graphene structure is observed to have a 3.4 Å crystalline spacing, thus having a high carrier mobility (Neto et al., 2009; Semeniuk et al., 2021a). The PL can be due to the band gap and recombination of electron–hole pairs in the π site of the sp2 hybridized carbons (Chien et al., 2012). The excitons in this structure have an infinite Bohr diameter, thus showing quantum confinement effects (Zhu et al., 2012). The presence of the sp2 hybridized lattice structure was confirmed by both Raman spectra and HRTEM. Fig. 1c shows the result of maple syrup HC. Impurities of oxygen, copper, aluminium, and silicon. The same elements are also present in the raw sap material.

The XRD pattern in Fig. 1d shows solidified and carbonized pure maple syrup. A characteristic peak of 26.4 and 38.6 show graphite (002) and carbonized sucrose (100) as calculated by the Debye Scherrer equation, which is used to determine the mean size of single crystal nanoparticles or crystalline in bulk materials using the equation for XRD (Holzwarth and Gibson, 2011; Patterson, 1939; Scherrer, 1912).
(2)
l =
K λ
β c o s θ
where l is the mean size of the ordered crystalline domain, K is the shape factor with a value of 0.9, λ is the wavelength of X-ray, β is the line broadening at full width half maxima in radian, and the Bragg angle is θ . The results here confirm the presence of graphite clusters. The analysis indicates that the microstructure of HC maple syrup changes the kind of disorder in graphite after hydrothermal carbonization, and this is verified by diffraction pattern in Fig. 1d. During the hydrothermal carbonization, sucrose from syrup degrades to a graphitic structure (Fig. 1e).

Results obtained from FTIR demonstrate the aromaticity of carbon by the presence of the CC and CO aromatic stretching at 1715 cm−1 and 1609 cm−1 (Fig. 2a). An aromatic CCH in-plane bend is observed at 1212 cm−1, 1060 cm−1, and 1025 cm−1. The slight absorption peak observed at 2942 cm−1, 2882 cm−1, and 2816 cm−1 relate to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of -CH2 bond. The broad hydroxyl peak at 3414 cm−1 signifies the hydrogen bonded phenol.

The UV–Visible spectra of HC maple syrup suspended in an NMP solution shows a maximum absorbance peak of 276 nm and a prolonged tail in the visible region (Fig. 2b). The peak in this area arose as a result of n – π * transition of electrons for the CO bond, and the π – π * transition of electrons of the conjugated CC band (Mintz et al., 2021).

The bandgap of semiconducting materials can be obtained from optical absorbance measurements using the Tauc method:
(3)
( α ⋅ h v )
2
= β
( h v −
E
g
)
where h is the Planck constant, v is photon’s frequency, β is the constant and
E
g
the band gap. α is the absorption coefficient determined by the Beer–Lambert law (Zanatta, 2019; Ali et al., 2020; Talasila et al., 2020). The band gap was determined to be 0.81 eV.

The emission spectra of HC maple syrup shows photoluminescence at 450 nm, when excited with 355 nm UV light. The quantum yield (QY) of this hydrothermally treated maple syrup is 5.9% compared to maple sap, which has a QY of 1%.

The photoluminescent properties were further studied to obtain applications in biological and environmental fields. Here, the obtained HC maple syrup was used as a fluorescent probe for detection of Fe3＋ ions (Fig. 3a). Concentration of 2000 μ M were used to determine the excellent selectivity of Fe3＋ ions, contrasted with Cu2＋, K＋, Fe2＋, Cr3＋, and Ca＋, which observed no significant fluorescence quenching changes. This shows high selectivity of Fe3＋ ions due to the electrostatic interaction of Fe3＋ and the fluorophore. The electron is transferred from the electron donor, or fluorophore, to the Fe3＋ ion, thus preventing radiative recombination of excitons.

The potential interference ions were also tested to check the selectivity of HC maple syrup towards different ions. Common ions tested were Cu2＋, K＋, Fe2＋, Cr3＋, and Ca＋ at the same concentrations as Fe3＋. The significant decay of PL intensity was seen with Fe3＋, and no significant difference with Cu2＋, K＋, Fe2＋, Cr3＋, or Ca＋ ions.

For an ion sensitivity study, different concentrations of Fe3＋ was studied from 62.5 μ M to 2000 μ M. Fig. 3b shows as Fe3＋ concentration increases, the ability of HC maple syrup to sense Fe3＋ ions also increases. Fig. 3c shows the relationship between ion concentration and PL intensity. After adding 2000 μ M Fe3＋ ions, the fluorescence is 97% quenched.

The fluorescence quenching data follows the function of the Stern–Volmer Method (Lu et al., 2012).
(4)
F
0
F
− 1 = K c
where,
F
0
is the PL intensity in the absence of ions, F is the PL intensity in the presence of ions, K is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and c is the concentration of the ion. The Stern–Vomer​ plot shows a correlation between PL intensity and the concentration of Fe3＋ in the range of 0.5 μ M to 500 μ M (Fig. 3d).

The pH of the solution was observed to change the emission intensity following a sigmoidal titration curve, with pH of 1 quenching the solution and pH 11 to 13 increasing the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3e). The addition of HCl or NaOH changes the surface functional groups charge state (Kong et al., 2014). Fluorescence quenching can be seen due to protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups, initiating fluorescence (Gao et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021). Decreasing pH protonates the carboxylic acid and oxygen group, thus quenching fluorescence (Supplementary Information). The photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process is inhibited at low pH (Ye et al., 2019). Increasing the pH deprotonates carboxylic acid groups, where a negative charge is formed on the surface and has a lower non-radiative recombination rate (Zheng et al., 2015). The alkaline conditions also restore some of the zigzag edges in graphene, where the bandgap transitions from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), σ to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), π
(Lin and Zhang, 2012). Theoretically, the rate of energy transfer depends on 3 factors: orientation, extent of overlap between the donor and acceptor, and separation distance between the donor and acceptor.

In computing, a logic gate is a device that takes inputs and produces an output (Brown, 2007; Zhai et al., 2018; Zhao and Chen, 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). Logic gates provide a solution to a problem by implementing functions to accomplish a precise task. The outputs can be single, combinations, sequential, and reversible systems. The fluorophore logic gate mimics electronic circuits by performing Boolean functions. NOT, AND and NOR logic gates were fabricated with spectral photoluminescent characteristics of the HC maple syrup as an output. Fe3＋ and pH inputs were used to create the various logic gates. The output signal is considered to be ON, with a binary value of 1 when the solution is fluorescent, and OFF with a binary value of 0 when the solution is quenched. This creates a chemo sensor, which is comparable to an on and off switch. When sensing for Fe3＋ ions, a NOT logic function is created. An AND function is created when sensing many pH inputs between pH 2 and 14. Another NOT function is created when the carbon is sensing hydroxyl ions. A NOR gate was created with 2 inputs and 1 output. Low pH was the first input. Fe3＋ was the second input (Table 1).

The only ON state is when input 1 is OFF, meaning pH is greater than 1, and input 2 is OFF, meaning Fe3＋ ions are not present. A low pH of 1 quenches the solution, giving an output value of 0. These gates, when combined, can create complex logic systems. With 2 of these NOR gates, an SR latch is created (Fig. 4a). An SR latch is a memory device (Fig. 4b). The device can easily be switched ON and OFF based on the pH of the solution, a reversible response.

These chemo logic gates are advantageous to metal–oxide–semiconductor field-effect transistor (MOSFET) based logic gates and nano regime electronic circuits, since they provide unique inputs compared to the traditional voltage-only information (Wright et al., 2020). These chemo sensor devices based on logic gates will act as a smart material and will help further develop smart nanodevices.

In this work, we developed a novel, cost-effective process to synthesize graphitic carbon with desired impregnated elements, based on hydrothermal treatment of xylem sap. This process eliminates the need to use hazardous synthetic chemicals during synthesis. This novel crystalline sp2 hybridized carbon was shown to selectively detect pH and Fe3＋ ions for both environmental and biomedical purposes. NOT, AND, and NOR logic gates were designed for applications as an on and off switch, a chemo sensor, and a memory device. Chemo sensor devices based on logic gates will act as smart materials and will help further develop smart nanodevices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank you to Ontario Research Fund – Research Excellence- 07, Abhinav Mohan, and Yixin Ren from the University of Toronto, and to Prof. Raynald Gauvin and Nicolas Brodusch from McGill University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.egyr.2022.02.124.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
Video S1
Fluorescence quenching.
